scenarioTypes.name: Brand New ARIA Build
scenarioTypes.description: A new RO site implementing ARIA as a new OIS build.

scenarioTypes.name: MOSAIQ to ARIA Conversion
scenarioTypes.description: A site moving from MOSAIQ to ARIA.

scenarioTypes.name: ARIA Upgrade / Optimization
scenarioTypes.description: An existing ARIA customer upgrading or optimizing workflows.

scenarioTypes.name: Consolidation to Target ARIA Site
scenarioTypes.description: Multiple sites adopting a target ARIA environment.

phases.title: Project Discovery & Initiation
phases.purpose: Understand the project type, sold scope, customer expectations, stakeholders, and early risks.
checklist: Review Unity project record and related work orders
Review purchased consulting services and scope
Review software and environment planning documents
Review interoperability planning document if applicable
Review customer account history and prior trip reports if available
Review project timeline and major milestones with Project Manager
Meet with PM to understand schedule, blockers, and customer expectations
Meet with OSM / Sales to understand customer goals and account history
Identify project type: new ARIA build, conversion, upgrade, or consolidation
Identify current OIS and future OIS state
Identify key stakeholders and decision makers
Identify interface analyst / IT contacts if applicable
Identify potential early risks and assumptions
Document initial questions for kickoff / discovery call
phases.tip: Discovery is the first chance to prevent rework.

observations.title: Scope does not match expectations
notice: The quote includes interoperability consulting, but kickoff notes suggest the customer expects full workflow redesign and additional onsite training.
The PM summary does not mention the mismatch.
The customer wants to go live quickly.
observations.attention: This catches your attention because scope gaps become customer-confidence problems if they are not addressed early.
questions: What does the customer believe was sold?
Who should clarify scope before kickoff?
observations.senior: The customer is not necessarily wrong; expectations may simply be misaligned. A CIC should surface the gap early with the PM/OSM before it becomes a customer escalation.
good: Confirm interop ownership and testing expectations.
Prepare expectation-setting language for the kickoff.
context: Quote and kickoff notes do not align.

observations.title: Project path is unclear
notice: Installed base indicates existing ARIA, but Sales notes reference a new build.
An OSM note mentions a recently acquired site.
The handoff does not clearly say whether this is new build, upgrade, conversion, or consolidation.
observations.attention: This matters because the project path changes the discovery questions, configuration strategy, training plan, and go-live risk.
questions: Is this new ARIA, conversion, upgrade, or consolidation?
Are we preserving, redesigning, or standardizing workflows?
observations.senior: Experienced CICs classify the project before they start planning. If you assume the wrong project type, every downstream decision can be off.
good: Document current and future OIS state.
Ask whether a target-site workflow exists.
Adjust the phase plan based on project type.

phases.title: Pre-On Site Assessment
phases.purpose: Prepare the customer, agenda, stakeholders, and artifacts so OSA produces usable workflow information.
checklist: Attend project kickoff call if scheduled
Explain consulting role, services, and expected outcomes
Determine customer goals and success criteria
Determine project type and customer expectations
Determine ROAPM / MIPS / QPP or reporting requirements if applicable
Establish communication preferences and primary points of contact
Identify customer project lead and decision makers
Identify required stakeholder groups for OSA
Identify Super Users or functional leaders if known
Request current-state workflow documents and screenshots
Request task lists, reports, and work queues if applicable
Request Charge Master / procedure codes if applicable
Request relevant Data Admin / configuration screenshots if applicable
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit if applicable
Begin implementation guide shell / notes structure
phases.tip: Good OSA planning protects the quality of the onsite visit.

observations.title: Customer wants one-day OSA
notice: The director asks if the OSA can be compressed into one day.
The proposed attendee list excludes nursing/navigation and billing.
The interface analyst is not invited.
observations.attention: This matters because a shorter OSA may still be workable, but only if required outcomes are protected.
questions: Which workflows are at risk if time is reduced?
Who must be present for decisions?
Can some interviews happen virtually before or after onsite?
observations.senior: Do not make this a fight about days. Make it about outputs: workflows, stakeholders, interface needs, configuration decisions, and training readiness.
good: Document risk if the customer compresses the assessment.

observations.title: Hidden stakeholder appears
notice: A nurse navigator asks whether she should attend the OSA.
She mentions tracking referrals and follow-ups in a spreadsheet.
She was not on the original stakeholder list.
observations.attention: This catches your attention because hidden stakeholders often own important upstream or downstream workflows.
questions: Where should it live in the future state?
What breaks if this workflow is missed?
observations.senior: New consultants often interview only the obvious roles. Experienced CICs look for the people who hold the department together operationally.

phases.title: On Site Assessment
phases.purpose: Observe real operations, interview stakeholders, collect evidence, and uncover hidden workflows.
checklist: Tour the department and observe patient flow
Observe nursing / navigator workflow if applicable
Observe chart QA / peer review workflow if applicable
Interview billing / charge capture stakeholders if applicable
Interview IT / interface analyst if applicable
Record interviewee names, roles, and contact information
Collect screenshots, documents, reports, and current-state artifacts
Identify shadow systems such as whiteboards, spreadsheets, paper logs, sticky notes
Identify naming conventions and standardization issues
Identify workflow risks and open decisions
Review findings with customer during wrap-up
Discuss next steps and proposed consulting plan
Complete trip report / work order documentation
Obtain Acknowledgement of Services signature if applicable
phases.tip: Observe first. Diagnose second. Recommend third.

observations.title: Whiteboard beside treatment console
notice: You walk into the treatment area and see a dry erase board beside the console.
The board includes imaging reminders, setup shifts, physician-specific notes, and special treatment considerations.
Therapists appear to update it throughout the day.
observations.attention: This process was not mentioned during discovery and does not appear in the documents you reviewed.
questions: What happens if it is erased?
observations.senior: The board is not the issue. The issue is the workflow need that created it.
good: Determine whether ARIA already supports the need.

observations.title: Navigator spreadsheet
notice: The navigator maintains an Excel tracker for referral status, authorization status, consult readiness, and follow-up reminders.
The tracker is updated daily.
Other staff rely on the navigator for status updates.
observations.attention: This may be a shadow workflow that supports patient movement before consult or after treatment.
questions: Is this duplicated in Epic or ARIA?
What happens when the navigator is out?
observations.senior: Shadow trackers often exist because the formal workflow does not meet an operational need.

phases.title: Implementation Guide
phases.purpose: Turn findings into future-state workflows, configuration direction, and customer-approved decisions.
checklist: Map physics / dosimetry / chart QA workflow
Identify configuration decisions and open items
Insert relevant screenshots or process maps
Review guide internally with manager / mentor if needed
Confirm guide approval / alignment before configuration
phases.tip: The guide is the bridge from observation to build.

observations.title: Physicians disagree on standardization
notice: One physician wants fewer documentation steps.
Another wants standardized templates for every consult.
The director wants consistency for reporting and training.
observations.attention: This matters because uncontrolled physician variation can make configuration, training, and support harder.
questions: How will this affect training and support?
observations.senior: Standardization is not rigidity. The goal is governed variation that the customer can sustain.
good: Tie decisions to configuration and training.

phases.title: Configuration Preparation
phases.purpose: Confirm environment, access, tools, and dependencies before configuration work begins.
checklist: Confirm configuration dates, attendees, and location
Confirm TBox / test environment availability if applicable
Confirm production environment availability if applicable
Confirm ARIA login access for consultant and customer users
Confirm SmartConnect / remote connection if applicable
Confirm MS Word and required tools are installed
Confirm Adobe / PDF tools if applicable
Confirm Word Developer Tab if document/template work is needed
Confirm eRx / SureScripts dependencies if applicable
Meet with Interface Analyst to discuss purchased interfaces and timelines
Confirm TBox configuration requirements for interface testing
Confirm hardware / printer / scanner dependencies if applicable
Communicate readiness blockers to PM / customer
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit if applicable
phases.tip: Configuration success is often decided before onsite work begins.

observations.title: Citrix not ready
notice: Two days before configuration, IT says ARIA and Data Admin are not published in Citrix.
The agenda assumes working access.
The PM was not aware access was still open.
observations.attention: This matters because onsite time can be wasted if environment readiness is assumed instead of validated.
questions: Can access be validated before travel?
What can still be done if access is delayed?
observations.senior: Do not confuse scheduled travel with readiness. Escalate blockers with options.

phases.title: TBox Configuration
phases.purpose: Build interface-dependent configuration needed for testing.
checklist: Understand objectives and purpose of TBox configuration
Review implementation guide and configuration tracker
Review pre-design configuration and best practices
Configure hospitals and departments as applicable
Configure waiting room / location resources if applicable
Configure signatures / approval workflows if needed
Configure care path components needed for testing if applicable
Document build decisions and open items
Close / complete work order if applicable
Obtain Acknowledgement of Services signature if applicable
phases.tip: TBox teaches whether design supports testing.

observations.title: Activity codes changed after mapping
notice: The Epic analyst reports that activity codes changed after initial mapping.
The customer says the new names are easier for scheduling.
Test scripts still show the old values.
observations.attention: This matters because build changes can affect mapping, test scripts, training, and retesting.
observations.senior: Configuration changes after mapping are not just build changes. They are testing changes.

phases.title: Production Configuration
phases.purpose: Configure production based on approved workflow decisions and build standards.
checklist: Understand objectives and purpose of production configuration
Review pre-design configuration and best practices
Confirm staff members appear in correct groups
Configure procedure codes / activity capture items
Configure tasks / workflow routing as applicable
Configure plan preparation / treatment management items as applicable
Configure reports / quick reports if applicable
Validate build with customer functional leaders
Close / complete work order if applicable
Obtain Acknowledgement of Services signature if applicable
phases.tip: Production configuration becomes training, support, reporting, and adoption.

observations.title: Naming convention disagreement
notice: Therapy wants short activity names.
Scheduling wants patient-friendly names.
Billing wants names aligned to charges.
Physicians only care if the name changes their workflow.
observations.attention: This matters because naming affects training, support, reporting, and sometimes interfaces.
questions: Does it affect charges or interfaces?
What standard can be supported long-term?
observations.senior: Naming is not cosmetic. It is a governance and support decision.

phases.title: Interface Testing
phases.purpose: Validate that interface behavior supports real workflows.
checklist: Understand overall objectives and purpose of interface testing
Confirm interface analyst / customer IT contacts
Validate ADT / registration workflow if applicable
Validate scheduling / appointment messages if applicable
Validate document outbound workflow if applicable
Validate charges / activity capture export if applicable
Validate imaging / PACS / TPS dependencies if applicable
Confirm expected workflow behavior in ARIA
Identify whether issues are message, mapping, configuration, workflow, or training related
Document defects with owner and priority
Communicate interface readiness risks to PM / customer
Confirm interfaces are ready for designated milestones
Update implementation guide / risk notes as needed
phases.tip: Test the workflow, not just the message.

observations.title: ADT works but appointment missing
notice: Epic creates the patient in ARIA successfully.
The expected appointment does not appear correctly.
The customer immediately says the interface is broken.
observations.attention: This matters because many interface issues are actually mapping, configuration, or test-case issues.
questions: Does ARIA configuration support the appointment?
Was the test case written correctly?
observations.senior: Do not escalate until you understand whether the defect is message, mapping, configuration, or workflow.

phases.title: EMR Training Preparation
phases.purpose: Prepare logistics, access, agendas, super users, and readiness before training begins.
checklist: Confirm dates, times, attendees, and room bookings
Ensure onsite training does not conflict with other events
Ensure computers have ARIA / Citrix access
Ensure MS Word and MS Excel availability
Ensure dedicated staff resources and training time
Identify Super Users / functional leaders for larger sites
Confirm beam configuration is complete for Eclipse customers if applicable
Check correct resources in staff members appear available within the department
Confirm eRx provider service levels updated with SureScripts in Data Admin if applicable
Confirm Varian Service Portal access if applicable
Confirm all users can log into production database
Confirm necessary users can log into test database
Make sure staff members appear within correct group
Ensure interfaces are ready to be turned on at designated milestones
Ensure printers and scanners are setup through Citrix, accessible, and tested
Ensure shared network folder is available and tested
Ensure camera access through Citrix is setup and tested
Ensure devices connected and tested: voice recognition, RadCalc, SunNuclear, etc. if applicable
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit

observations.title: Only half the staff can attend
notice: The director says clinic volume is too high to send all staff to training.
Super users are not finalized.
Physicians have not confirmed attendance.
observations.attention: This matters because training gaps usually become go-live issues.
questions: Can training be split by role?
observations.senior: Training attendance is not a courtesy. It is a go-live dependency.

phases.title: EMR Training
phases.purpose: Train users on role-specific workflows and reveal remaining adoption or configuration issues.
checklist: Understand overall objectives and purpose of EMR training
Conduct EMR training with Applications Specialists
Train user login / access / Active Directory understanding
Train User Home setup and functionality
Train View Settings: columns, data, etc.
Train Imaging / Image Browser / Offline Review if applicable
Train DICOM Import / Export if applicable
Debrief with customer to review training performed and next steps
Close / complete work order in Unity if applicable
Complete trip report on work order

observations.title: Physician skips training
notice: A senior physician says he will learn during go-live.
Staff are uncomfortable correcting physician workflow in production.
The director asks if this is a problem.
observations.attention: This matters because physician workflow gaps can block documentation, orders, prescriptions, and approvals.
questions: Can a focused session be offered?
What breaks if the physician is not trained?
observations.senior: Offer a focused mitigation, then document unresolved readiness risk.

phases.title: EMR Go-Live
phases.purpose: Support first production workflows, triage issues, assign owners, and protect patient operations.
checklist: Understand overall objectives and purpose of EMR Go Live
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit
Confirm 3rd party vendors are aware and included as necessary
Confirm interfaces tested and live in production
Confirm CT scanners can send images to PACS or Treatment Planning System if applicable
Confirm Applications Specialists are aware of Go Live plan
Coordinate onsite arrival time with customer
Confirm consults are scheduled for EMR Go Live week if applicable
Confirm CT simulations are scheduled for EMR Go Live week if applicable
Confirm printers / scanners / shared folders / cameras as needed
Provide onsite support with consult workflow
Provide onsite support with CT simulation workflow
Provide onsite support with treatment planning care paths if applicable
Maintain issue list with owner and priority
Debrief customer to review next steps
Close / complete work order in Unity if applicable
Complete trip report on work order

observations.title: Login failures and document routing
notice: Two users cannot log in.
Documents are not routing outbound to Epic.
A consult patient arrives in 20 minutes.
Staff ask whether they should go back to paper.
observations.attention: This matters because unstructured go-live issues can quickly damage confidence and patient flow.
observations.senior: The CIC should not become the only problem solver. The CIC helps structure triage and ownership.

phases.title: Plan Preparation & Cutover
phases.purpose: Validate active patient readiness before treatment go-live.
checklist: Understand overall objectives and purpose of cutover and plan preparation
Ensure all patients on treatment and active planning status are prepared for Treatment Go Live
Confirm customer understands cutover weekend and responsibilities
Confirm patients registered in HIS / EMR system
Confirm ADT triggered to send patients to ARIA if applicable
Confirm all plans are in Eclipse if applicable
Import plans from 3rd party into Eclipse / ARIA if applicable
Change new ARIA plan to Planning Approved status as appropriate
Schedule remaining fractions and imaging in Plan Scheduling
Add prescription in Prescribe Treatment and save as draft if appropriate
Manually treat fractions delivered in prior system in Plan Scheduling if applicable
Attach manually treated fractions to correct date if applicable
Change plan status to Treatment Approved when appropriate
Confirm physicians enter diagnosis in ARIA
Confirm physicians approve prescriptions in Prescribe Treatment
Confirm patient appointments are added as of go-live date
Confirm copy of prior prescription if applicable
Confirm setup notes and photos entered into Treatment Preparation
Move information from paperless chart / prior system into ARIA Journal Notes as applicable
Confirm any additional information necessary to treat patient safely
Print Dose-Site Summary after final treatment on Friday if applicable
Consult with Applications Specialist on Go-Live plan
Confirm Applications Specialist will review Treatment Administration with physics / system administrator if applicable
Confirm Applications Specialist will conduct 4DiTC workshops if applicable
Confirm Applications Specialist will support patient treatment delivery and imaging
Confirm Offline Review workshops if applicable
Confirm Chart QA workshops if applicable
Confirm Activity Capture workshops if applicable
Confirm exported charges review if applicable
Confirm all users can access ARIA
Ensure first day of Treatment Go Live is lightly scheduled
Confirm remaining interfaces moved to production
Book travel for Treatment Go Live
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit
Confirm 3rd party vendors are aware and included as necessary
Confirm interfaces tested and live in production

observations.title: Missing prescription and setup photos
notice: A patient scheduled after cutover has a treatment-approved plan but prescription approval is missing.
Setup photos are not in ARIA.
Therapists assume this will be handled before treatment.
observations.attention: This matters because readiness must be verified patient by patient before treatment delivery.
questions: Can this patient be treated safely?
observations.senior: Readiness is not a feeling. It is verified.

phases.title: Treatment Go-Live
phases.purpose: Support safe treatment delivery and final transition to production treatment workflows.
checklist: Understand overall objectives and purpose of Treatment Go Live
Prepare final version of implementation guide to present to customer
Include predicted post-implementation utilization scorecard if applicable
Upload final copy to Unity > Account if applicable
Confirm with SW Installer that machine cutover was successful
Confirm patient readiness board / transfer spreadsheet complete
Confirm first day schedule is appropriate
Support activity capture / charge review if applicable
Track go-live issues with owner and priority
Communicate with Project Manager after first patient treated successfully
Escalate critical patient safety or workflow blockers
Debrief with customer to review next steps
Close / complete work order in Unity if applicable
Complete trip report on work order
phases.tip: Never dismiss a clinical safety concern at the machine.

observations.title: Prescription and plan naming mismatch
notice: The physicist notices a mismatch between prescription naming and plan naming.
Therapists are ready to proceed.
The team is waiting at the machine.
observations.attention: This matters because a clinical safety concern at the machine should pause the workflow until validated.
questions: Is this cosmetic or clinically meaningful?
observations.senior: At the machine, the safest decision is to pause and validate.

phases.title: Post-Go-Live Optimization
phases.purpose: Review adoption, utilization, closeout, and optimization opportunities.
checklist: Schedule follow-up with customer 4-6 weeks post Treatment Go Live
If using Consulting Package, notify Ops Manager of final event so work order can be completed
Ensure all ACs for project are accounted for
Contact customer to arrange follow-up agenda
Create and send agenda if applicable
Review Consulting Report and predicted ARIA utilization
Use Chatter / internal communication to announce visit if applicable
Provide onsite follow-up support as needed
Contact Sales and OSM if customer could benefit from additional consulting or training
Close / complete work order in Unity if applicable
Complete trip report on work order
phases.tip: Post-go-live is where implementation becomes adoption.

observations.title: Low Care Path usage
notice: Care Path usage is low.
Physician documentation remains inconsistent.
Staff say they understand ARIA but reverted to familiar habits during busy clinics.
observations.attention: This matters because technical go-live is not the same as operational adoption.
questions: Is it training, configuration, workflow design, or leadership reinforcement?
observations.senior: Utilization findings should lead to a recommendation, not just a report.
good: Involve OSM/Sales if additional support is beneficial.
